sections.h2: "Family First" & "Family Is Everything" — Arabic Tattoo Phrases
intro: Beyond the single word, Arabic tattoo searchers reach for four core value phrases about family. Below is the standard Arabic rendering of each — verify with a native speaker before inking, but these are the accepted forms across Arabic-speaking countries.

image.alt: A multi-word Arabic phrase tattoo in Diwani calligraphy on a back — example of the length and flow of a full Arabic tattoo phrase

blocks.html: العائلة أولاً (Al-aila awwalan — Family first). Three words: العائلة (Al-aila — the family) + أولاً (awwalan — first). The most-searched Arabic family value phrase — reads well as a horizontal forearm or ribcage piece, or stacked down the spine.

blocks.h3: Family Is Everything — العائلة كل شيء
blocks.html: العائلة كل شيء (Al-aila kullu shay — Family is everything). Four words: العائلة (the family) + كل شيء (kullu shay — everything). A longer statement — best on the ribcage, upper back, or as a two-line design where العائلة sits above كل شيء.

blocks.h3: Family Is Forever — العائلة إلى الأبد
blocks.html: العائلة إلى الأبد (Al-aila ila al-abad — Family is forever). Four words with the poetic إلى الأبد (ila al-abad — until eternity). Suits Nastaliq or Diwani for the flowing final word — a common phrase in memorial family tattoos.

blocks.h3: I Love My Family — أحب عائلتي
blocks.html: أحب عائلتي (Uhibbu aailati — I love my family). Two words, direct and personal. Also common: أحب أمي (Uhibbu ummi — I love my mother), أحب أبي (Uhibbu abi — I love my father). See the wider love arabic tattoo guide for related love phrases.

sections.h2: Mother, Mom & Dad in Arabic Tattoo — Parent Tributes
intro: Parent tributes are the second-largest cluster of family Arabic tattoo searches, after the word "family" itself. Below is how each parent word actually reads in Arabic — and why the two-letter versions have stayed the most-tattooed family words worldwide.

image.alt: Ab (father) Arabic word tattoo in Naskh calligraphy on a ribcage — a compact father tribute

blocks.h3: Mother in Arabic Tattoo — أم / الأم / أمي
blocks.html: أم (Umm — mother) is the two-letter classic. الأم (Al-umm — the mother) is the definite form with more visual weight. أمي (Ummi — my mother) is the personal possessive. All three are common tattoos; أم fits any small placement, أمي reads warmer for a personal piece.

blocks.h3: Mom in Arabic Tattoo — ماما / أمي
blocks.html: For the informal English "mom," Arabic uses either ماما (Mama — the universal child-language word) or أمي (Ummi — my mother, warmer register). ماما is what most Arabic-speaking children actually call their mother — a genuinely warm choice, not just a translation.

blocks.h3: Dad in Arabic Tattoo — أب / بابا / أبي
blocks.html: أب (Ab — father) is the formal two-letter classic. بابا (Baba — dad, child-language) is the informal warm equivalent of ماما. أبي (Abi — my father) is the personal possessive.

blocks.h3: Mom and Dad in Arabic Tattoo — أم وأب / ماما وبابا
blocks.html: Paired parent tattoos work beautifully in Arabic — the symmetry of أم (Umm) and أب (Ab) as a two-piece design (one on each wrist, or stacked vertically) is a classic. Informal pairing: ماما وبابا (Mama wa Baba — Mom and Dad).

sections.h2: Sister in Arabic Tattoo — Sibling Tribute Words
intro: Sister and sibling Arabic tattoos are the smaller but distinct fourth cluster of family-tattoo searches. Below are the words for sister and brother, common paired combinations, and the writing choices sisters most often make for matching tattoos.

image.alt: Ukht (sister) Arabic word tattoo in Thuluth calligraphy on a ribcage

blocks.html: أخت (Ukht — sister) is the standard three-letter word. أختي (Ukhti — my sister) adds the personal possessive. Both fit compact placements: the inner wrist, ribcage, or behind the ear. Popular in matching-sister tattoos where both siblings get the same word.

For matching-sister tattoo designs (each sister gets one), the plural أخوات (Akhawat — sisters) or the shared word أختي (Ukhti — my sister) works on both. Common variations: one sister gets الأخت الكبرى (the older sister), the other gets الأخت الصغرى (the younger sister). Placement on matching wrists or ankles reads as an intentional pair.

أخ (Akh — brother) is the two-letter classic — the shortest Arabic family word and one of the most-tattooed. أخي (Akhi — my brother) is the personal form. For a sister-and-brother pair, أخت (sister) and أخ (brother) work as a symmetric two-piece design.

faq.q: How do you write "family" in Arabic for a tattoo?
faq.a: عائلة (Aila) is the standard modern Arabic word for family — four letters, universally recognized. For a personal possessive, use عائلتي (Aailati — my family). For an older, more poetic register, use أهل (Ahl — kin, people, family). All three are native-speaker verified and available in the generator above.

faq.q: What are the shortest Arabic family words for a tattoo?
faq.a: The four two-letter family words are the smallest Arabic tattoos of any category: أم (Umm — mother), أب (Ab — father), أخ (Akh — brother). Three-letter siblings include أخت (Ukht — sister), أهل (Ahl — kin), جدة (Jidda — grandmother). These fit any placement — behind the ear, inner finger, ankle, or as a single mark on the wrist.

faq.q: What Arabic phrase means "family first" for a tattoo?
faq.a: العائلة أولاً (Al-aila awwalan) — three words meaning "family first." Word breakdown: العائلة (the family) + أولاً (first). It's the standard, universally-accepted rendering. Related value phrases: العائلة كل شيء (Family is everything), العائلة إلى الأبد (Family is forever). Best rendered in Thuluth for weight or Nastaliq for a softer feel.

faq.q: Should I get "mom" and "dad" as separate Arabic tattoos or together?
faq.a: Both work. Separate: أم (Umm) on one wrist and أب (Ab) on the other creates a symmetric two-piece parent tribute — one of the most-tattooed Arabic family designs. Together: أم وأب (Umm wa Ab — Mom and Dad) sits as a compact two-word line on the forearm, ribcage, or ankle. For an informal warm version, use ماما وبابا (Mama wa Baba).

faq.q: What Arabic word means "sister" for a tattoo?
faq.a: أخت (Ukht) is the standard three-letter word for sister. أختي (Ukhti — my sister) adds the personal possessive. For matching-sister tattoo designs, both siblings can get the same word (أختي), or split it into الأخت الكبرى (the older sister) and الأخت الصغرى (the younger sister). The plural أخوات (Akhawat — sisters) also works for a group of three or more.

faq.q: Are family Arabic tattoos better in Thuluth or Nastaliq?
faq.a: Both work; the choice depends on tone. Thuluth gives family words ceremonial weight — best for reverent single-word tributes (أم, أب, عائلة) and religious-adjacent value phrases. Nastaliq gives family words a softer, more personal feel — best for the possessive forms (أمي, عائلتي) and longer phrases like العائلة إلى الأبد (Family is forever). Naskh is the safest choice when maximum legibility matters.
